      Shadow trading
   =20
 =20
Insider trading, I like to say, is not about fairness: It=E2=80=99s about t=
heft. In the US, most of the time, it is illegal to trade on inside informa=
tion about a company not because that=E2=80=99s unfair to everyone else who=
 doesn=E2=80=99t have the information, but because you have some=C2=A0duty=
=C2=A0to somebody else not to misuse their information. So if you work for =
a public company, you have a duty to the shareholders not to trade on insid=
e information before disclosing it to them. Or if you are the spouse or gol=
f buddy or therapist of an executive at a public company, and she tells you=
 about an upcoming deal, you have a =E2=80=9Cduty of trust or confidence=E2=
=80=9D to her not to go trade on it, and if you do that=E2=80=99s a crime.B=
ut if you don=E2=80=99t have a duty to keep the information confidential, p=
robably you can go ahead and trade on it. (Nothing here is legal advice!) S=
ome important, though somewhat hazy and controversial,=C2=A0examples:=09If =
you are an old friend of an executive at a public company, and you go out t=
o drinks with her and she tells you =E2=80=9Cugh I=E2=80=99ve been working =
so hard, we=E2=80=99re getting acquired,=E2=80=9D probably you have a duty =
to keep that information confidential, and if you trade on it that=E2=80=99=
s a crime. But if you are just some random person sitting at the next table=
 and you=C2=A0overhear her saying that, you have no duty of trust and confi=
dence to her, and you can probably trade.=09If a corporate raider or strate=
gic acquirer or Warren Buffett is planning to propose a takeover of a publi=
c company, and you are working on the deal, you have a duty to the acquirer=
 to keep the deal confidential, and if you go out and buy some call options=
 that=E2=80=99s a crime. But if the acquirer=C2=A0itself quietly buys some =
stock =E2=80=94=C2=A0a =E2=80=9Ctoehold=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0before maki=
ng the proposal public, that=E2=80=99s probably fine: The acquirer has no i=
nside information=C2=A0about the company; it only has inside information=C2=
=A0about its own plans, and can do what it wants with those. Warren Buffett=
=E2=80=99s Berkshire Hathaway Inc. is allowed to buy a stake in a company a=
nd then announce that stake, rather than the reverse; it is allowed to trad=
e knowing its own intentions.=C2=A0=09In fact, if the acquirer=C2=A0wants=
=C2=A0to share that information with somebody else, so that=C2=A0they=C2=A0=
will buy shares to support its bid, it can probably do that, though this on=
e is more controversial. (In particular it does not work with tender offers=
.) But the acquirer=E2=80=99s information belongs to it, and if it wants to=
=C2=A0give it away, insider trading law can=E2=80=99t stop it.Again, not le=
gal advice.Last month, the US Securities and Exchange Commission won an ins=
ider trading case=C2=A0against Matthew Panuwat, who worked at a public comp=
any called Medivation Inc., which was acquired by Pfizer Inc. in 2016. Panu=
wat found out about the deal ahead of time, and did=C2=A0not=C2=A0trade Med=
ivation stock. (That would be illegal!) Instead, he bought call options on =
Incyte Corp., a competitor to Medivation, apparently on the theory that whe=
n the Medivation deal was announced Incyte=E2=80=99s stock would also go up=
. It did, and he made money. The SEC argued that (1) he had material nonpub=
lic information=C2=A0about Incyte=C2=A0(something like =E2=80=9Cits competi=
tor would be acquired and its stock would go up=E2=80=9D), (2) he got that =
information=C2=A0from Medivation=C2=A0and (3) he had a duty to Medivation=
=C2=A0to keep it confidential and not trade on it. Therefore, the SEC said,=
 it was illegal insider trading =E2=80=94=C2=A0theft of Medivation=E2=80=99=
s information =E2=80=94=C2=A0for him to trade Incyte options.=C2=A0And a ju=
ry agreed. This =E2=80=94=C2=A0trading one stock using inside information a=
bout another stock =E2=80=94 is often called =E2=80=9Cshadow trading.=E2=80=
=9DWhere did the SEC get the idea that Panuwat had a duty to Medivation to =
keep this information about Incyte confidential? Well, because that was his=
 explicit agreement with Medivation. It was in Medivation=E2=80=99s insider=
 trading policy, which said:During the course of your employment ... you ma=
y receive important information that is not yet publicly disseminated ... a=
bout the Company. =E2=80=A6 Because of your access to this information, you=
 may be in a position to profit financially by buying or selling or in some=
 other way dealing in the Company=E2=80=99s securities ... or the securitie=
s of another publicly traded company, including all significant collaborato=
rs, customers, partners, suppliers, or competitors of the Company. ... For =
anyone to use such information to gain personal benefit ... is illegal.Medi=
vation=E2=80=99s policies said that he was not allowed to use information h=
e got about Medivationto trade other stocks.=C2=A0Therefore, the SEC argued=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0and a judge and jury agreed =E2=80=94=C2=A0when Panuwat did=
 that, he was violating the policy; he was stealing information from Mediva=
tion. Therefore he was guilty of insider trading under federal law.Fine. Bu=
t that was just Medivation=E2=80=99s choice.=C2=A0If Medivation=E2=80=99s p=
olicy hadn=E2=80=99t had the =E2=80=9Cor the securities of another publicly=
 traded company=E2=80=9D bit, then I=C2=A0think=C2=A0the SEC would not have=
 had a case. (Still not legal advice! And I think the SEC disagrees.
  [1]
)=C2=A0I wrote last month:Not every company has a policy like that; some ha=
ve policies saying, for instance, =E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t use inside informa=
tion to trade our stock,=E2=80=9D without mentioning competitors. For=C2=A0=
all=C2=A0I=C2=A0know=C2=A0some companies might=C2=A0encourage=C2=A0their ex=
ecutives to trade competitors=E2=80=99 stocks. The meaning of Panuwat might=
 be something like =E2=80=9Cshadow trading is illegal if your company tells=
 you it is.=E2=80=9DI will say, when I wrote that, I heard from a number of=
 readers to the effect of =E2=80=9Cactually=C2=A0most companies have a poli=
cy like that.=E2=80=9D For instance, here are a paper and related blog post=
 by Geeyoung Min of Michigan State University finding that =E2=80=9Capproxi=
mately seventy-six percent of the disclosed, stand-alone insider trading po=
licies from S&P 500 companies prohibit the trading of any other publicly tr=
aded companies=E2=80=99 stock based on MNPI [material nonpublic information=
].=E2=80=9D And here is a client memo from Cleary Gottlieb, written shortly=
 after the Panuwat verdict, saying that =E2=80=9Ccompanies should consider =
expanding their insider trading policies to expressly prohibit trading in t=
heir own securities and securities of others that could be impacted by MNPI=
 acquired in the=C2=A0course of their employment.=E2=80=9DBut =E2=80=A6=C2=
=A0why? Medivation was not=C2=A0actually harmed=C2=A0by Panuwat=E2=80=99s t=
rading in Incyte=E2=80=99s stock.
  [2]
=C2=A0Perhaps, as a matter of, like, fairness, or being friendly with the S=
EC, companies really should have policies saying =E2=80=9Cyou can=E2=80=99t=
 shadow trade=C2=A0in our competitors=E2=80=99 stocks.=E2=80=9D But perhaps=
 as a matter of employee relations (and keeping the employees out of troubl=
e), companies should instead have policies saying =E2=80=9Cgo ahead and sha=
dow trade in our competitors=E2=80=99 stocks, that is explicitly allowed by=
 our policies, have fun.=E2=80=9D Why would a company want to have a policy=
 that could send its employees to jail for doing something that doesn=E2=80=
=99t hurt the company?At Bloomberg Law today, Matthew Bultman reports:The W=
all Street regulator=E2=80=99s victory, likely to eventually reach a federa=
l appeals court, is forcing companies to take a second look at their own in=
sider trading policies. Some have expanded those policies to explicitly add=
ress shadow trading, according to a Bloomberg Law search. Others are consid=
ering narrowing their prohibitions, hoping to limit employees=E2=80=99 expo=
sure. =E2=80=A6Now, some companies are considering narrowing their policies=
, wary of exposing their employees to similar liability.=E2=80=9CFrom the S=
EC=E2=80=99s perspective, that is perhaps an unintended consequence of the =
Panuwat case,=E2=80=9D [Morrison & Foerster LLP lawyer Edward] Imperatore s=
aid.But other companies have already moved in the opposite direction, under=
scoring the difficult decisions firms face.At least some insider trading po=
licies now include the term =E2=80=9Cshadow trading=E2=80=9D and reference =
the=C2=A0Panuwat=C2=A0case, a Bloomberg Law review of company disclosures f=
ound.The theory of narrowing the policy seems fairly straightforward: Why g=
et your employees in trouble unnecessarily? The theory of broadening the po=
licy is something like =E2=80=9Cwell, if the employees shadow trade, the SE=
C is going to go after them anyway, and you want to look like you have=C2=
=A0 effective policies against insider trading so the SEC doesn=E2=80=99t g=
et mad at you too=E2=80=9D:Companies can be liable for insider trading by t=
heir employees, and may have viewed the broad language as a way to minimize=
 potential liability, [Geeyoung] Min said. If regulators or prosecutors cam=
e knocking, companies could argue their policies went beyond what was requi=
red. ...Lawyers who urge companies to explicitly restrict shadow trading in=
 their policies note there may have been other reasons Panuwat found himsel=
f in hot water: the SEC also argued he had a duty to avoid trading on the m=
erger news, even absent Medivation=E2=80=99s policy, because the company en=
trusted him with confidential information.But I am not so sure; I do kind o=
f think that the right reading of Panuwat is that it=E2=80=99s illegal if t=
he policy says it is and not if it doesn=E2=80=99t. Which, sure,=C2=A0is we=
ird:Under that logic, whether a person=E2=80=99s trade is illegal may depen=
d on what=E2=80=99s in their employer=E2=80=99s insider trading policy, leg=
al scholars said.=E2=80=9CThat does seem to give companies too much discret=
ion to make a special law for themselves and themselves only,=E2=80=9D said=
 Columbia law professor John Coffee, whose specialties include securities r=
egulation and white collar crime. =E2=80=9CThe criminal law doesn=E2=80=99t=
 like the idea of different scopes depending upon the decision of the corpo=
rate issuer.=E2=80=9DYes, in general, it is odd to have criminal law that c=
ompanies get to make for themselves. But that=E2=80=99s how insider trading=
 law works!

I mean: Yesterday was the day when the new T+1 settlement system went into =
effect in the US stock market, which means that today, one business day aft=
er yesterday, is the day that all those T+1 trades settle. How=E2=80=99re w=
e doing?=C2=A0Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Greg Ritchie reports:The proportion of US=
 securities transactions failing to settle remained largely steady on Wedne=
sday, as Wall Street aced the first major test of its move to a faster trad=
ing system.Data=C2=A0released=C2=A0by the Depository Trust & Clearing Corp.=
 show the =E2=80=9CFails Rate=E2=80=9D recorded in its Continuous Net Settl=
ement system =E2=80=94 a platform that aims to minimize the exchange of sec=
urities between counterparties by netting off trades =E2=80=94 was 1.90% on=
 Wednesday. That compares to a daily average of 2.09% last week, before new=
 rules halved the time allowed to complete every transaction to a single da=
y.For matched trades processed outside of CNS the fails rate was 2.92% vers=
us a 3.35% average last week, the DTCC data show.The DTCC sits at the heart=
 of the US market and processes the vast majority of all trades, making the=
 figures the first major indicator of the impact of the transition to what=
=E2=80=99s known as T+1. The industry consensus was that a rise in trade fa=
ilures was likely as firms adjusted to the new settlement cycle.Last week, =
US stock trades settled T+2, meaning that if you sold stock on Monday, you =
delivered the stock and received the cash on Wednesday. Starting this week,=
 they settle T+1, so if you sold stock yesterday you deliver it today.=C2=
=A0The general expectation about the move to T+1 was that most trades would=
 settle in one day, but the fail rate would go up: More trades would not se=
ttle on time, because traders had trouble finding or recalling stock borrow=
 or converting their foreign currencies into dollars in just one business d=
ay. =E2=80=9CMoving from T+2 to T+1 settlement means something more like mo=
ving from T+2.001 settlement to, like, T+1.05 settlement,=E2=80=9D I wrote =
last week.
  [3]
 Settling stock trades is not just about moving digits around in databases;=
 it requires actual commercial activity, and shortening the settlement cycl=
e means that sometimes that activity won=E2=80=99t be completed in time.But=
 so far =E2=80=94=C2=A0a few hours into the T+1 experience =E2=80=94=C2=A0T=
+1 seems to have a=C2=A0lower=C2=A0fail rate than T+2 did.=C2=A0Some possib=
le hypotheses for why:=09If you work in the settlement bits of a big broker=
 or bank, and in March you went to your boss to ask to take off Memorial Da=
y week, she said =E2=80=9Cabsolutely not it=E2=80=99s all hands on deck tha=
t week.=E2=80=9D Everyone is paying attention to settlements this week, in =
a way that they did not last week, so all the best people are working on it=
 with total focus. Eventually the fail rate will creep up to where it was b=
efore, or higher, but for now the system is operating at peak effectiveness=
.=09Possibly there is a more general and permanent form of that explanation=
: The transition to T+1 forced a lot of banks and brokerages to upgrade the=
ir systems and processes, so that they are now not only=C2=A0faster=C2=A0bu=
t also=C2=A0better=C2=A0at settling trades, so the fail rate should go down=
 permanently.=09Conversely, if you are a trader who is in the business of d=
oing gnarly trades that have a risk of failing =E2=80=94=C2=A0shorting hard=
-to-borrow stocks, maybe, or converting illiquid currencies into dollars ac=
ross time zones to buy US stocks =E2=80=94=C2=A0maybe you=C2=A0did=C2=A0tak=
e this week off. =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99m gonna let them work out the kinks in =
the system before I try to borrow DJT stock for T+1 settlement,=E2=80=9D ma=
ybe you thought, and you went on vacation this week. Maybe the fail rate is=
 lower this week because people are only doing the easy trades.=09Maybe set=
tling T+1 really is easier and less error-prone? One theoretical problem wi=
th T+2 settlement =E2=80=94=C2=A0one=C2=A0reason=C2=A0that the system switc=
hed to T+1 =E2=80=94=C2=A0is that, with T+2 settlement, trades are=C2=A0at =
risk=C2=A0for two days. =E2=80=9CTime equals risk,=E2=80=9D the SEC said in=
 proposing the switch to T+1; =E2=80=9Cless time between a transaction and =
its completion reduces risk.=E2=80=9D If you agree a trade on Monday and se=
ttle it on Wednesday, and disaster strikes on Tuesday afternoon, the trade =
might not settle; if you settle T+1 then more trades will settle. Of course=
 it=E2=80=99s not like a lot of disasters were striking last week; last wee=
k=E2=80=99s 2.09% fail rate is probably not explained by, like, widespread =
counterparty bankruptcies. Still maybe when people have two days to settle =
their trades, they =E2=80=A6=C2=A0forget about them? There is just more tim=
e for glitches to occur, so more glitches occur. Whereas with T+1 settlemen=
t you just do the trade and settle it, with less time to mess it up.=C2=A0

 duty to the shareholders not to trade on inside information before disclos=
ing it to them. Or if you are the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbus=

=C2=A0before making the proposal public, that=E2=80=99s probably fine: The =

<div id=3D"footnote-2" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[2] Could you tell a story? Something like =E2=
=80=9CPanuwat=E2=80=99s buying of Incyte options, if it was big enough, cou=
ld have pushed up the price of Incyte, which would have alerted the market =
to possible deal activity in the sector, which could have pushed up the pri=
ce of Medivation stock, which could have made Pfizer get nervous and walk a=
way from the deal=E2=80=9D? This strikes me as not crazy, in the general ca=
se, though clearly not true in Panuwat's specific case. But the point is th=
at the company gets to decide how worried it is about stuff like this.</p>
